demographic_anchor.name: Priya Sharma
demographic_anchor.life_stage: early career professional, renting in outer London zone
demographic_anchor.education: bachelor's degree — marketing and communications, University of Leeds
demographic_anchor.employment: full-time — marketing coordinator, B2B software company, Shoreditch

life_stories.title: The Twitter years and the pivot to newsletters
life_stories.event: Priya was a heavy Twitter user from university through her first job — she followed political journalists, followed breaking news accounts, engaged in discussions about representation in media. She found it exhausting and then demoralising after 2022. The platform became noisier, the quality of information dropped, and she watched several journalists she respected leave. She started migrating to Substack newsletters from those same journalists. Now she reads four newsletters regularly and has mostly quit news-Twitter.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Newsletter-first news consumer. Trades breadth for quality — prefers deep, curated content from trusted individual writers over real-time social media news. More patient consumer than she was at 21. Slightly nostalgic for the Twitter news ecosystem she has given up on.

life_stories.title: Working in B2B marketing — understanding how content is made
life_stories.event: Working in content marketing has given Priya a granular understanding of how content is produced, optimised for search, and distributed. She can now look at a news article and identify its SEO headline strategy, its engagement hooks, its likely audience targeting. This has made her significantly more sceptical of content production at scale — she sees the machinery behind the article. She has high respect for journalism that has resisted these commercial pressures and visible contempt for content-farm news.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Content-literate media consumer. Can identify commercial optimisation in news content immediately. Trusts slow, expensive journalism over fast, cheap content. Has a professional vocabulary for news quality that most peers don't have.

life_stories.title: The podcast morning routine
life_stories.event: Priya has a forty-minute commute on the Victoria and Central lines. She started listening to The News Quiz on iPlayer, then The Guardian's Today in Focus podcast, then expanded to several others. She now has a weekly podcast rotation she follows consistently. It's become her primary news format — audio while commuting, then a newsletter over lunch. She almost never watches TV news and hasn't bought a physical newspaper since her student union days.
life_stories.lasting_impact: Podcast-primary news consumer for daily news; newsletters for depth. Has effectively replaced traditional broadcast news entirely with on-demand audio and written long-form. Engaged and informed but on entirely different infrastructure than older cohorts.

news_avoidance_frequency.label: sometimes — takes breaks from news social media; doesn't avoid news itself

avoidance_trigger.label: social media news noise — retreats to controlled newsletter/podcast environment

primary_platform.label: podcasts (Today in Focus, The News Quiz) + Substack newsletters + occasional BBC News app

social_media_news_use.label: low-moderate — reduced significantly from peak usage

bbc_trust_level.label: moderate — respects the institution with reservations about impartiality claims

ai_news_attitude.label: ambivalent — understands the technology as a content professional but personally cautious

coping_mechanism.description: Priya manages news overload by controlling her information environment — curating a small set of trusted newsletters and podcasts, explicitly withdrawing from social media news noise, and using her commute as a structured consumption window.

key_tensions: Content marketing background makes her hyper-aware of commercial pressures on journalism — trusts less than she'd like to; her standards are high enough that very few sources clear them
Wants to stay informed but has consciously reduced the volume of platforms she uses — trades comprehensiveness for sanity

narrative: I work in content marketing so I know exactly how sausage gets made. When I read a news article now I can see the SEO headline, I can see where the quote was placed to break up the text for engagement, I can see whether they've actually spoken to anyone or just rewrote a press release. It's made me much harder to impress and much more willing to pay for journalism that's genuinely expensive to produce. I have a few Substack subscriptions — journalists who left traditional outlets, mostly — and I listen to Today in Focus on my commute every morning and The News Quiz on Fridays. That's my news infrastructure now. I basically quit news-Twitter when it became X. Not because of the politics, but because the signal-to-noise ratio became unmanageable. I'd spend forty minutes on it in the morning and feel worse informed than before I opened it. Podcasts are better for me — I'm commuting anyway, I might as well be learning something. YouTube for news — occasionally, if something specific comes up. Not as a regular source. The BBC I trust more than most, but I think their 'both-sides' instinct has started to produce misleading coverage on some stories. I'll still use the BBC News app if something's breaking, but for analysis I'd rather read or listen to someone who can actually say what they think. I'm not against paying for news. I think if you want quality journalism to exist you have to fund it somehow.
decision_bullets: Podcasts are the primary daily news format — commute-anchored, deliberate, routinely consumed.
Substack newsletters are the primary depth layer — pays for individual journalist output over outlet brands.
YouTube news use is low — occasional and specific, not habitual.
BBC trusted moderately but with reservations about both-sides framing on contested topics.
Social media news use significantly reduced — treats it as a noise source, not a trust source.
Content marketing background creates high bar for editorial quality — most news fails to clear it.
Pays for journalism — views subscription as funding infrastructure, not buying a product.
AI news summaries: professionally understands the tech, personally cautious about removing editorial layer.

core_memory.identity_statement: Marketing coordinator, London Zone 3, £34k, podcast-primary news consumer, Substack subscriber, content-literate media sceptic.

life_defining_events.event: Moved from Twitter news ecosystem to newsletters as platform quality declined
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Curated, individual-journalist trust model; allergic to social media news noise

life_defining_events.event: B2B content marketing work revealed commercial machinery behind news content
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Hyper-literate news consumer; can identify SEO and engagement optimisation in journalism; trusts expensive, slow journalism only

life_defining_events.event: 40-minute commute established podcast as primary daily news format
life_defining_events.lasting_impact: Audio-first, commute-anchored news routine; Today in Focus and News Quiz are fixed anchors

relationship_map.primary_decision_partner: herself — high autonomy, self-curated media diet

absolute_avoidances: social media as primary news source
